context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{theorem}[Theorem \ref{thm: alg- functor} and Proposition \ref{prop:inj}]\label{thm: intro} Let $\mathcal{X}$ be the quiver algebroid stack obtained by gluing the Maurer-Cartan deformation spaces of a collection of Lagrangian immersions $\mathcal{L}$, using isomorphisms in the (extended) Fukaya category. Then there exists an $A_\infty$ functor $$\mathscr{F}^\mathcal{L}: \mathrm{Fuk} (M) \xrightarrow{} \mathrm{Tw}(\mathcal{X}),$$ where $\mathrm{Tw}(\mathcal{X})$ is the category of twisted complexes over $\mathcal{X}$. Furthermore, $\mathscr{F}^\mathcal{L}$ is injective on $\mathrm{HF}^\bullet((\mathcal{L}',b_0),L)$ for any Lagrangian $L$ and any constant elements $b_0$ in the deformation space of $\mathcal{L}'$, where $\mathcal{L}'$ is a subset of $\mathcal{L}$. \end{theorem}
definitions: Let $\mathcal{X}$ be the quiver algebroid stack obtained by gluing the Maurer-Cartan deformation spaces of a collection of Lagrangian immersions $\mathcal{L}$, using isomorphisms in the (extended) Fukaya category.
let((\mathcal{L}',b_0),L)$ for any Lagrangian $L$ and any constant elements $b_0$ in the deformation space of $\mathcal{L}'$, where $\mathcal{L}'$ is a subset of $\mathcal{L}$.
local_assumptions: if the moduli functors for some problems fail to be representable due to the existence of automorphisms, it may still be possible to construct a moduli stack.

Conclusion: [Free projective space] \label{ex:freeProj} Consider the quiver $Q$ with two vertices $0,1$ and several arrows $a_k, k=0,\ldots,n$ from vertex $0$ to $1$. context_pack.section: A brief description and an example of a quiver stack
context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{example}[Free projective space] \label{ex:freeProj} Consider the quiver $Q$ with two vertices $0,1$ and several arrows $a_k, k=0,\ldots,n$ from vertex $0$ to $1$. An affine chart of the path algebra $\mathbb{C} Q$ can be constructed by localizing $\mathbb{C} Q$ at one arrow $a_l$ for $l=0,\ldots,n$. We take $A' = \mathbb{C} Q'$ where $Q'$ is the quiver with a single vertex and $n$ loops $X_k, k\in \{0,\ldots,n\}-\{l\}$. We fix the image vertex of $G_{01}$ to be the vertex $0$. Then define \begin{align*} G_{01}(X_k) =& a_l^{-1}a_k; \, G_{10}(a_k) = X_k\\ c(0)=&0; \, c(1)=a_l^{-1}. \end{align*} One can easily check that the required equations are satisfied. In particular, the gerbe terms arise naturally. This is a free algebra analog of the projective space, where $a_k,X_k$ are the homogeneous and inhomogeneous coordinates. \end{example}
local_assumptions: If one understands a path algebra as a category, where objects are vertices and morphisms are arrows, then a representation $G$ is a functor preserving the relations.

theorem thm_associativity {s p q r : ℕ} (w : G q r (X r) → X q) :
    (c s p q) ∘ (G s p) (G p q w) ∘ (c s p q)⁻1 = G s q w ∧
    (c s p q) ∘ (G s p) (G p q w) ∘ (G s p) (c p q r)⁻1 ∘ (c s p r)⁻1 = (G s q w) ∘ (c s q r)⁻1

	we have

	In particular,

entries.lean_statement: theorem ex_nclocP2 (Q : Type*) (R : Ideal (PathAlgebra ℂ Q)) (Rℏ : Ideal (PathAlgebra ℂ Q)) (hR : R = Ideal.span {Path.mk (a2 * b1) - Path.mk (b2 * a1), Path.mk (a3 * b2) - Path.mk (b3 * a2), Path.mk (a1 * b3) - Path.mk (b1 * a3)}) (hRℏ : Rℏ = Ideal.span {Path.mk (a3 * b2 - Complex.exp ℏ * b3 * a2) * Path.mk c1, Path.mk (a1 * b3 - Complex.exp ℏ * b1 * a3) * Path.mk c2, Path.mk (a2 * b1 - Complex.exp ℏ * b2 * a1) * Path.mk c3}) : ∃ (A Aℏ : Type*) (hA : Ring A) (hAℏ : Ring Aℏ) (hAlgebraA : Algebra ℂ A) (hAlgebraAℏ : Algebra ℂ Aℏ), A ≃a[ℂ] PathAlgebra ℂ Q ⧸ R ∧ Aℏ ≃a[ℂ] PathAlgebra ℂ Q ⧸ Rℏ ∧ (∀ (a2 b1 : A), a2 * b1 = Complex.exp ℏ * b2 * a1)

semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Assumptions: [NC local projective plane as an algebra] \label{ex:nclocP2} Consider the quiver $Q$ given on the right of Figure \ref{fig:C3Q3}.; We have the quiver algebra $\mathbb{A}=\mathbb{C} Q/R$, where the ideal $R$ are generated by $a_2b_1 - b_2a_1$ and other similar relations, which are the cyclic derivatives of the spacetime superpotential $$(a_3b_2-b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-b_2a_1)c_3.$$ $\mathbb{A}$ is derived equivalent to the total space of the canonical line bundle $X=K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$ \cite{BKR01,vdBergh}, which is the crepant resolution of the orbifold $\mathbb{C}^3/\mathbb{Z}_3$.; The simplest one is given by the following deformation of the spacetime superpotential: \begin{equation} (a_3b_2-e^{\hbar}b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-e^{\hbar}b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-e^{\hbar}b_2a_1)c_3.; \end{equation} For instance, this gives the commuting relation $a_2 b_1= e^\hbar b_2a_1$.; One of the relations take the form $p(\hbar) a_2 b_1 + q(\hbar) b_2a_1 + r(\hbar) c_2 c_1$, where $(p(\hbar),q(\hbar),r(\hbar))$ is given by theta functions and produces an embedding of an elliptic curve in $\mathbb{P}^2$. Conclusion: $\mathbb{A}$ admits interesting noncommutative deformations. Let's denote the resulting algebra by $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$. Indeed, Sklyanin algebras \cite{AS87,ATV} provide an even more interesting class of deformations of $\mathbb{A}$. Such deformations were constructed in \cite{CHL-nc} using mirror symmetry.

extracted_assumptions: [NC local projective plane as an algebra] \label{ex:nclocP2} Consider the quiver $Q$ given on the right of Figure \ref{fig:C3Q3}.

We have the quiver algebra $\mathbb{A}=\mathbb{C} Q/R$, where the ideal $R$ are generated by $a_2b_1 - b_2a_1$ and other similar relations, which are the cyclic derivatives of the spacetime superpotential $$(a_3b_2-b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-b_2a_1)c_3.$$ $\mathbb{A}$ is derived equivalent to the total space of the canonical line bundle $X=K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$ \cite{BKR01,vdBergh}, which is the crepant resolution of the orbifold $\mathbb{C}^3/\mathbb{Z}_3$.
The simplest one is given by the following deformation of the spacetime superpotential: \begin{equation} (a_3b_2-e^{\hbar}b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-e^{\hbar}b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-e^{\hbar}b_2a_1)c_3.
\end{equation} For instance, this gives the commuting relation $a_2 b_1= e^\hbar b_2a_1$.

One of the relations take the form $p(\hbar) a_2 b_1 + q(\hbar) b_2a_1 + r(\hbar) c_2 c_1$, where $(p(\hbar),q(\hbar),r(\hbar))$ is given by theta functions and produces an embedding of an elliptic curve in $\mathbb{P}^2$.
semantic_equivalence_artifact.extracted_conclusion: $\mathbb{A}$ admits interesting noncommutative deformations. Let's denote the resulting algebra by $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$. Indeed, Sklyanin algebras \cite{AS87,ATV} provide an even more interesting class of deformations of $\mathbb{A}$. Such deformations were constructed in \cite{CHL-nc} using mirror symmetry.

The simplest one is given by the following deformation of the spacetime superpotential: \begin{equation} (a_3b_2-e^{\hbar}b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-e^{\hbar}b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-e^{\hbar}b_2a_1)c_3.
\end{equation} For instance, this gives the commuting relation $a_2 b_1= e^\hbar b_2a_1$.

One of the relations take the form $p(\hbar) a_2 b_1 + q(\hbar) b_2a_1 + r(\hbar) c_2 c_1$, where $(p(\hbar),q(\hbar),r(\hbar))$ is given by theta functions and produces an embedding of an elliptic curve in $\mathbb{P}^2$.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{example}[NC local projective plane as an algebra] \label{ex:nclocP2} Consider the quiver $Q$ given on the right of Figure \ref{fig:C3Q3}. We have the quiver algebra $\mathbb{A}=\mathbb{C} Q/R$, where the ideal $R$ are generated by $a_2b_1 - b_2a_1$ and other similar relations, which are the cyclic derivatives of the spacetime superpotential $$(a_3b_2-b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-b_2a_1)c_3.$$ $\mathbb{A}$ is derived equivalent to the total space of the canonical line bundle $X=K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$ \cite{BKR01,vdBergh}, which is the crepant resolution of the orbifold $\mathbb{C}^3/\mathbb{Z}_3$. $\mathbb{A}$ admits interesting noncommutative deformations. The simplest one is given by the following deformation of the spacetime superpotential: \begin{equation} (a_3b_2-e^{\hbar}b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-e^{\hbar}b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-e^{\hbar}b_2a_1)c_3. \end{equation} For instance, this gives the commuting relation $a_2 b_1= e^\hbar b_2a_1$. Let's denote the resulting algebra by $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$. Indeed, Sklyanin algebras \cite{AS87,ATV} provide an even more interesting class of deformations of $\mathbb{A}$. Such deformations were constructed in \cite{CHL-nc} using mirror symmetry. One of the relations take the form $p(\hbar) a_2 b_1 + q(\hbar) b_2a_1 + r(\hbar) c_2 c_1$, where $(p(\hbar),q(\hbar),r(\hbar))$ is given by theta functions and produces an embedding of an elliptic curve in $\mathbb{P}^2$. \end{example}

assumptions: [NC local projective plane as an algebra] \label{ex:nclocP2} Consider the quiver $Q$ given on the right of Figure \ref{fig:C3Q3}.

We have the quiver algebra $\mathbb{A}=\mathbb{C} Q/R$, where the ideal $R$ are generated by $a_2b_1 - b_2a_1$ and other similar relations, which are the cyclic derivatives of the spacetime superpotential $$(a_3b_2-b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-b_2a_1)c_3.$$ $\mathbb{A}$ is derived equivalent to the total space of the canonical line bundle $X=K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$ \cite{BKR01,vdBergh}, which is the crepant resolution of the orbifold $\mathbb{C}^3/\mathbb{Z}_3$.
The simplest one is given by the following deformation of the spacetime superpotential: \begin{equation} (a_3b_2-e^{\hbar}b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-e^{\hbar}b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-e^{\hbar}b_2a_1)c_3.
\end{equation} For instance, this gives the commuting relation $a_2 b_1= e^\hbar b_2a_1$.

One of the relations take the form $p(\hbar) a_2 b_1 + q(\hbar) b_2a_1 + r(\hbar) c_2 c_1$, where $(p(\hbar),q(\hbar),r(\hbar))$ is given by theta functions and produces an embedding of an elliptic curve in $\mathbb{P}^2$.
translation_statement_schema.claim: $\mathbb{A}$ admits interesting noncommutative deformations. Let's denote the resulting algebra by $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$. Indeed, Sklyanin algebras \cite{AS87,ATV} provide an even more interesting class of deformations of $\mathbb{A}$. Such deformations were constructed in \cite{CHL-nc} using mirror symmetry.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[NC local projective plane as a quiver stack] \label{ex:algst}
	Consider three copies of noncommutative $\mathbb{C}^3$ \eqref{eq:ncC3}, denoted by $\mathcal{A}^{\tilde{\hbar}}_i$ for $i=1,2,3$, which correspond to the three corners of the polyhedral set as shown in Figure \ref{fig:ncKP2}. Later, we will see that they are the nc deformation spaces of some immersed Lagrangians.  We use $(x_1,y_1,w_1)$, $(y_2,z_2,w_2)$ and $(z_3,x_3,w_3)$ to denote their generating variables.
	
	We glue these three copies of nc $\mathbb{C}^3$ with localizations of the quiver algebra 
	$$\mathcal{A}_0^\hbar := \mathbb{A}^\hbar=\mathbb{C} Q/R^\hbar$$ 
	given in Example \ref{ex:nclocP2}, where the left-right ideal $R^\hbar$ is generated by the cyclic derivatives of 	$(a_3b_2-e^{\hbar}b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-e^{\hbar}b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-e^{\hbar}b_2a_1)c_3$.  (For instance, $b_1c_3 = e^{\hbar} c_1b_3$, by taking cyclic derivative in $a_2$.)
	
	We take the localizations $$\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{01}):=\mathbb{A}^\hbar\langle a_1^{-1},a_3^{-1} \rangle,\, \mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{02}):=\mathbb{A}^\hbar\langle c_1^{-1},c_3^{-1} \rangle,\, \mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{03}):=\mathbb{A}^\hbar\langle b_1^{-1},b_3^{-1} \rangle.$$
	Here, $U_{03}$ denote the neighborhoods of the corners of the base polytope, so that the union of $U_{0i}$ for $i=1,2,3$ equals to the polytope.
	
	For the gluing direction $\mathcal{A}_i^{\tilde{\hbar}} \to \mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i})$, we take the homomorphisms defined by:
	
	
	\begin{equation}
		G_{01}:\begin{cases}
			x_1 \mapsto c_1a_1^{-1} \\
			y_1 \mapsto b_1a_1^{-1} \\
			w_1 \mapsto a_1a_3a_2;
		\end{cases}
		G_{02}:\begin{cases}
			y_2 \mapsto b_1c_1^{-1}\\
			z_2 \mapsto a_1c^{-1}_1\\
			w_2 \mapsto c_1c_3c_2;
		\end{cases}
		G_{03}:\begin{cases}
			z_3 \mapsto a_1b_1^{-1}\\
			x_3 \mapsto c_1b^{-1}_1\\
			w_3 \mapsto b_1b_3b_2.
		\end{cases}
		\label{eq:G0j}
	\end{equation}
	It can be checked explicitly that the above is a homomorphism, once we set 
	$$\tilde{\hbar} = -3\hbar.$$
	For instance, $x_1y_1 - e^{-3\hbar} y_1x_1=0$ is sent to $c_1a_1^{-1}b_1a_1^{-1} - e^{-3\hbar}b_1a_1^{-1}c_1a_1^{-1}=0$.
	
	However, for the reverse direction, there is no algebra homomorphism $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i}) \to \mathcal{A}^{\tilde{\hbar}}_i$.  Thus the gluing cannot make sense using algebra homomorphisms.  Rather, we need to use representations of $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i})$ over $\mathcal{A}^{\tilde{\hbar}}_i$, see Definition \ref{def:rep}.
	
	We take the following representation of $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{03})$ by $\mathcal{A}_3^{\hbar}$:
	\begin{equation}
		G_{30}:\begin{cases}
			(a_1,b_1,c_1)\mapsto (z_3,1,x_3)\\
			(a_2,b_2,c_2)\mapsto (e^{\hbar}w_3z_3,w_3,e^{-\hbar}w_3x_3)\\
			(a_3,b_3,c_3)\mapsto (e^{-\hbar}z_3,1,e^{\hbar}x_3).\\
		\end{cases}
	\end{equation}
	The representations $G_{i0}$ of $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i})$ by $\mathcal{A}_i^{\hbar}$ for $i=2,1$ are obtained by cyclic permutation $(a,b,c) \mapsto (b,c,a) \mapsto (c,a,b)$ and $(z_3,x_3,w_3) \mapsto (y_2,z_2,w_2)\mapsto (x_1,y_1,w_1)$ respectively.
	
	It is easy to check that $G_{i0} \circ G_{0i} = \mathrm{Id}_{\mathcal{A}_i^\hbar}$.  However,
	$$ G_{0i} \circ G_{i0} \not= \mathrm{Id}_{\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i})}.$$
	In general, when $\mathcal{A}_0$ has more vertices than $\mathcal{A}_i$, such equality cannot hold simply because the representation of vertices is not a bijection.  For instance, 
	$$G_{03} \circ G_{30}(a_2) = e^{\hbar} (b_1b_3b_2)(a_1b_1^{-1}) = b_1b_3\cdot a_2 \not=a_2.$$
	
	Rather, we have
	$$ G_{0i} \circ G_{i0}(a) = c_{0i0}(h_a) G_{00}(a) c_{0i0}^{-1}(t_a) $$
	for all arrows $a$, if we set 
	\begin{align*}
		c_{030}(v_3) =& b_1b_3, c_{030}(v_1) = b_1, c_{030}(v_2) = e_2;\\
		c_{020}(v_3) =& c_1c_3, c_{020}(v_1) = c_1, c_{020}(v_2) = e_2;\\
		c_{010}(v_3) =& a_1a_3, c_{010}(v_1) = a_1, c_{010}(v_2) = e_2.\\	
	\end{align*}
	For instance,
	$$ G_{03} \circ G_{30}(a_3) = e^{-\hbar} a_1b_1^{-1} = b_1\cdot a_3\cdot (b_1b_3)^{-1}.$$
	Thus, gerbe terms $c_{0i0}$ are necessary for gluing quivers with different numbers of vertices.
	
	Now for any $i,j \in \{1,2,3\}$, we define
	$$ G_{ij} := G_{i0} \circ G_{0j} : \mathcal{A}_j(U_{ij}) \to \mathcal{A}_i(U_{ij}). $$
	The localizations $\mathcal{A}_j(U_{ij})$ are the standard toric ones and can be read from the polytope picture (Figure \ref{fig:ncKP2}).  Explicitly, $\mathcal{A}_1(U_{12}) = \mathcal{A}_1\langle x_1^{-1} \rangle$ and $\mathcal{A}_1(U_{13}) = \mathcal{A}_1\langle y_1^{-1} \rangle$.  The others $\mathcal{A}_2(U_{2j})$ and $\mathcal{A}_3(U_{3j})$ are obtained by the substitution $(x_1,y_1) \leftrightarrow (y_2,z_2) \leftrightarrow (z_3,x_3)$.
	
	Then we have
	$$ G_{ij} \circ G_{jk} (x) = G_{i0} \circ (G_{0j} \circ G_{j0}) \circ G_{0k}(x) = G_{i0}\left(c_{0j0}(h_{G_{0k}(x)}) \cdot G_{0k}(x) \cdot c_{0j0}^{-1}(t_{G_{0k}(x)})\right).$$
	Note that in our definition \eqref{eq:G0j} for $G_{0k}$, $G_{0k}(x)$ are loops at vertex 2 for all $x$.  Moreover, $c_{0j0}(v_2)=e_2$.  Hence $c_{0j0}(h_{G_{0k}(x)}) \cdot G_{0k}(x) \cdot c_{0j0}^{-1}(t_{G_{0k}(x)})=G_{0k}(x)$, and we obtain the cocycle condition
	$$ G_{ij} \circ G_{jk} = G_{ik}$$
	for any $i,j,k \in \{1,2,3\}$.  Explicitly, one can check that the gluing maps $G_{ij}$ are the one given in Figure \ref{fig:ncKP2}, producing the noncommutative local $\mathbb{P}^2$.  This is an example of a noncommutative toric variety.  Deformation quantizations of toric varieties were studied in \cite{CLS1,CLS2}.
	
	In summary, we obtain a quiver algebroid stack consisting of four charts, $\mathcal{A}_i$ for $i=0,1,2,3$.  If we forget the chart $\mathcal{A}_0$, then the remaining three charts glue up to an algebroid stack $X^{\tilde{\hbar}}$ that has trivial gerbe term, that is, a sheaf of algebras.  
	
	Interesting phenomena arise as we turn on $\hbar$, due to the existence of a compact divisor.  First, the deformation parameters of the algebra $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$ and the algebroid stack $X^{\tilde{\hbar}}$ are related in the non-trivial way
	$$\tilde{\hbar} = -3\hbar.$$
	Second, the toric gluing also needs to be deformed (by the factor $e^{-2\tilde{\hbar}}$ in this example) in order to satisfy the cocycle condition.
	
	%We claim that this algebroid stack is equivalent to the algebra $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$ in Example \ref{ex:nclocP2}.
	
	\begin{figure}[htb!]
		\centering
		\includegraphics[scale=0.6]{ncKP2.pdf}
		\caption{An algebroid stack which is a noncommutative deformation of $K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$.}
		\label{fig:ncKP2}
	\end{figure}
	
	%There are two interesting phenomena we observe here.  First, \emph{there is a factor of $3$ in the noncommutative deformation of $\mathbb{C}^3$-charts}, in order to match with the algebra $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$.  Second, there is a factor of $e^{-18\hbar}$ in the gluing between chart 2 and chart 3.  Such a factor is unavoidable because of \emph{monodromy} around the compact divisor, and they can also be understood via \emph{gerbes}.  It leads to \emph{non-trivial homotopy terms}.
	These non-trivial factors only manifest when we turn on the deformation $\hbar\not=0$.

However, for the reverse direction, there is no algebra homomorphism $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i}) \to \mathcal{A}^{\tilde{\hbar}}_i$.

We take the following representation of $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{03})$ by $\mathcal{A}_3^{\hbar}$: \begin{equation} G_{30}:\begin{cases} (a_1,b_1,c_1)\mapsto (z_3,1,x_3)\\ (a_2,b_2,c_2)\mapsto (e^{\hbar}w_3z_3,w_3,e^{-\hbar}w_3x_3)\\ (a_3,b_3,c_3)\mapsto (e^{-\hbar}z_3,1,e^{\hbar}x_3).\\ \end{cases} \end{equation} The representations $G_{i0}$ of $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i})$ by $\mathcal{A}_i^{\hbar}$ for $i=2,1$ are obtained by cyclic permutation $(a,b,c) \mapsto (b,c,a) \mapsto (c,a,b)$ and $(z_3,x_3,w_3) \mapsto (y_2,z_2,w_2)\mapsto (x_1,y_1,w_1)$ respectively.

For instance, $$G_{03} \circ G_{30}(a_2) = e^{\hbar} (b_1b_3b_2)(a_1b_1^{-1}) = b_1b_3\cdot a_2 \not=a_2.$$ Rather, we have $$ G_{0i} \circ G_{i0}(a) = c_{0i0}(h_a) G_{00}(a) c_{0i0}^{-1}(t_a) $$ for all arrows $a$, if we set \begin{align*} c_{030}(v_3) =& b_1b_3, c_{030}(v_1) = b_1, c_{030}(v_2) = e_2;\\ c_{020}(v_3) =& c_1c_3, c_{020}(v_1) = c_1, c_{020}(v_2) = e_2;\\ c_{010}(v_3) =& a_1a_3, c_{010}(v_1) = a_1, c_{010}(v_2) = e_2.\\ \end{align*} For instance, $$ G_{03} \circ G_{30}(a_3) = e^{-\hbar} a_1b_1^{-1} = b_1\cdot a_3\cdot (b_1b_3)^{-1}.$$ Thus, gerbe terms $c_{0i0}$ are necessary for gluing quivers with different numbers of vertices.

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
[NC local projective plane as a quiver stack] \label{ex:algst}
	Consider three copies of noncommutative $\mathbb{C}^3$ \eqref{eq:ncC3}, denoted by $\mathcal{A}^{\tilde{\hbar}}_i$ for $i=1,2,3$, which correspond to the three corners of the polyhedral set as shown in Figure \ref{fig:ncKP2}. Later, we will see that they are the nc deformation spaces of some immersed Lagrangians.  We use $(x_1,y_1,w_1)$, $(y_2,z_2,w_2)$ and $(z_3,x_3,w_3)$ to denote their generating variables.
	
	We glue these three copies of nc $\mathbb{C}^3$ with localizations of the quiver algebra 
	$$\mathcal{A}_0^\hbar := \mathbb{A}^\hbar=\mathbb{C} Q/R^\hbar$$ 
	given in Example \ref{ex:nclocP2}, where the left-right ideal $R^\hbar$ is generated by the cyclic derivatives of 	$(a_3b_2-e^{\hbar}b_3a_2)c_1 + (a_1b_3-e^{\hbar}b_1a_3)c_2 + (a_2b_1-e^{\hbar}b_2a_1)c_3$.  (For instance, $b_1c_3 = e^{\hbar} c_1b_3$, by taking cyclic derivative in $a_2$.)
	
	We take the localizations $$\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{01}):=\mathbb{A}^\hbar\langle a_1^{-1},a_3^{-1} \rangle,\, \mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{02}):=\mathbb{A}^\hbar\langle c_1^{-1},c_3^{-1} \rangle,\, \mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{03}):=\mathbb{A}^\hbar\langle b_1^{-1},b_3^{-1} \rangle.$$
	Here, $U_{03}$ denote the neighborhoods of the corners of the base polytope, so that the union of $U_{0i}$ for $i=1,2,3$ equals to the polytope.
	
	For the gluing direction $\mathcal{A}_i^{\tilde{\hbar}} \to \mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i})$, we take the homomorphisms defined by:
	
	
	\begin{equation}
		G_{01}:\begin{cases}
			x_1 \mapsto c_1a_1^{-1} \\
			y_1 \mapsto b_1a_1^{-1} \\
			w_1 \mapsto a_1a_3a_2;
		\end{cases}
		G_{02}:\begin{cases}
			y_2 \mapsto b_1c_1^{-1}\\
			z_2 \mapsto a_1c^{-1}_1\\
			w_2 \mapsto c_1c_3c_2;
		\end{cases}
		G_{03}:\begin{cases}
			z_3 \mapsto a_1b_1^{-1}\\
			x_3 \mapsto c_1b^{-1}_1\\
			w_3 \mapsto b_1b_3b_2.
		\end{cases}
		\label{eq:G0j}
	\end{equation}
	It can be checked explicitly that the above is a homomorphism, once we set 
	$$\tilde{\hbar} = -3\hbar.$$
	For instance, $x_1y_1 - e^{-3\hbar} y_1x_1=0$ is sent to $c_1a_1^{-1}b_1a_1^{-1} - e^{-3\hbar}b_1a_1^{-1}c_1a_1^{-1}=0$.
	
	However, for the reverse direction, there is no algebra homomorphism $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i}) \to \mathcal{A}^{\tilde{\hbar}}_i$.  Thus the gluing cannot make sense using algebra homomorphisms.  Rather, we need to use representations of $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i})$ over $\mathcal{A}^{\tilde{\hbar}}_i$, see Definition \ref{def:rep}.
	
	We take the following representation of $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{03})$ by $\mathcal{A}_3^{\hbar}$:
	\begin{equation}
		G_{30}:\begin{cases}
			(a_1,b_1,c_1)\mapsto (z_3,1,x_3)\\
			(a_2,b_2,c_2)\mapsto (e^{\hbar}w_3z_3,w_3,e^{-\hbar}w_3x_3)\\
			(a_3,b_3,c_3)\mapsto (e^{-\hbar}z_3,1,e^{\hbar}x_3).\\
		\end{cases}
	\end{equation}
	The representations $G_{i0}$ of $\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i})$ by $\mathcal{A}_i^{\hbar}$ for $i=2,1$ are obtained by cyclic permutation $(a,b,c) \mapsto (b,c,a) \mapsto (c,a,b)$ and $(z_3,x_3,w_3) \mapsto (y_2,z_2,w_2)\mapsto (x_1,y_1,w_1)$ respectively.
	
	It is easy to check that $G_{i0} \circ G_{0i} = \mathrm{Id}_{\mathcal{A}_i^\hbar}$.  However,
	$$ G_{0i} \circ G_{i0} \not= \mathrm{Id}_{\mathcal{A}_{0}^\hbar(U_{0i})}.$$
	In general, when $\mathcal{A}_0$ has more vertices than $\mathcal{A}_i$, such equality cannot hold simply because the representation of vertices is not a bijection.  For instance, 
	$$G_{03} \circ G_{30}(a_2) = e^{\hbar} (b_1b_3b_2)(a_1b_1^{-1}) = b_1b_3\cdot a_2 \not=a_2.$$
	
	Rather, we have
	$$ G_{0i} \circ G_{i0}(a) = c_{0i0}(h_a) G_{00}(a) c_{0i0}^{-1}(t_a) $$
	for all arrows $a$, if we set 
	\begin{align*}
		c_{030}(v_3) =& b_1b_3, c_{030}(v_1) = b_1, c_{030}(v_2) = e_2;\\
		c_{020}(v_3) =& c_1c_3, c_{020}(v_1) = c_1, c_{020}(v_2) = e_2;\\
		c_{010}(v_3) =& a_1a_3, c_{010}(v_1) = a_1, c_{010}(v_2) = e_2.\\	
	\end{align*}
	For instance,
	$$ G_{03} \circ G_{30}(a_3) = e^{-\hbar} a_1b_1^{-1} = b_1\cdot a_3\cdot (b_1b_3)^{-1}.$$
	Thus, gerbe terms $c_{0i0}$ are necessary for gluing quivers with different numbers of vertices.
	
	Now for any $i,j \in \{1,2,3\}$, we define
	$$ G_{ij} := G_{i0} \circ G_{0j} : \mathcal{A}_j(U_{ij}) \to \mathcal{A}_i(U_{ij}). $$
	The localizations $\mathcal{A}_j(U_{ij})$ are the standard toric ones and can be read from the polytope picture (Figure \ref{fig:ncKP2}).  Explicitly, $\mathcal{A}_1(U_{12}) = \mathcal{A}_1\langle x_1^{-1} \rangle$ and $\mathcal{A}_1(U_{13}) = \mathcal{A}_1\langle y_1^{-1} \rangle$.  The others $\mathcal{A}_2(U_{2j})$ and $\mathcal{A}_3(U_{3j})$ are obtained by the substitution $(x_1,y_1) \leftrightarrow (y_2,z_2) \leftrightarrow (z_3,x_3)$.
	
	Then we have
	$$ G_{ij} \circ G_{jk} (x) = G_{i0} \circ (G_{0j} \circ G_{j0}) \circ G_{0k}(x) = G_{i0}\left(c_{0j0}(h_{G_{0k}(x)}) \cdot G_{0k}(x) \cdot c_{0j0}^{-1}(t_{G_{0k}(x)})\right).$$
	Note that in our definition \eqref{eq:G0j} for $G_{0k}$, $G_{0k}(x)$ are loops at vertex 2 for all $x$.  Moreover, $c_{0j0}(v_2)=e_2$.  Hence $c_{0j0}(h_{G_{0k}(x)}) \cdot G_{0k}(x) \cdot c_{0j0}^{-1}(t_{G_{0k}(x)})=G_{0k}(x)$, and we obtain the cocycle condition
	$$ G_{ij} \circ G_{jk} = G_{ik}$$
	for any $i,j,k \in \{1,2,3\}$.  Explicitly, one can check that the gluing maps $G_{ij}$ are the one given in Figure \ref{fig:ncKP2}, producing the noncommutative local $\mathbb{P}^2$.  This is an example of a noncommutative toric variety.  Deformation quantizations of toric varieties were studied in \cite{CLS1,CLS2}.
	
	In summary, we obtain a quiver algebroid stack consisting of four charts, $\mathcal{A}_i$ for $i=0,1,2,3$.  If we forget the chart $\mathcal{A}_0$, then the remaining three charts glue up to an algebroid stack $X^{\tilde{\hbar}}$ that has trivial gerbe term, that is, a sheaf of algebras.  
	
	Interesting phenomena arise as we turn on $\hbar$, due to the existence of a compact divisor.  First, the deformation parameters of the algebra $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$ and the algebroid stack $X^{\tilde{\hbar}}$ are related in the non-trivial way
	$$\tilde{\hbar} = -3\hbar.$$
	Second, the toric gluing also needs to be deformed (by the factor $e^{-2\tilde{\hbar}}$ in this example) in order to satisfy the cocycle condition.
	
	%We claim that this algebroid stack is equivalent to the algebra $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$ in Example \ref{ex:nclocP2}.
	
	\begin{figure}[htb!]
		\centering
		\includegraphics[scale=0.6]{ncKP2.pdf}
		\caption{An algebroid stack which is a noncommutative deformation of $K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$.}
		\label{fig:ncKP2}
	\end{figure}
	
	%There are two interesting phenomena we observe here.  First, \emph{there is a factor of $3$ in the noncommutative deformation of $\mathbb{C}^3$-charts}, in order to match with the algebra $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$.  Second, there is a factor of $e^{-18\hbar}$ in the gluing between chart 2 and chart 3.  Such a factor is unavoidable because of \emph{monodromy} around the compact divisor, and they can also be understood via \emph{gerbes}.  It leads to \emph{non-trivial homotopy terms}.
	These non-trivial factors only manifest when we turn on the deformation $\hbar\not=0$.

If $$\psi_{ji}: \hat{G}_{ji}(C_i(U_{ij}))\rightarrow C_{j}(U_{ij})$$ is an $\mathcal{A}_{j}(U_{ij})$-module map and $(\psi_{ji})_\#$ is intertwining, then $\psi_{ji} = \widehat{(\psi_{ji})_\#}$. context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{prop} \label{prop:comp} Take any $0 \leq p<q \leq k$. Let $y^{(i)}, z^{(i)} \in \mathcal{A}_i(U)$ with $t_{y^{(i)}} = h_{z^{(i)}}$ for $i=0,\ldots,k$. Then the product $\mathcal{M}_{k,\ldots ,0}\left(y^{\left(k\right)}z^{\left(k\right)}\otimes \ldots \otimes y^{\left(0\right)}z^{\left(0\right)}\right)$ equals to the decomposition {\small \begin{equation*} \mathcal{M}_{k,\ldots ,q,p,\ldots ,0}\left(y^{\left(k\right)}z^{\left(k\right)}\otimes \ldots \otimes y^{\left(q\right)}\otimes \mathcal{M}_{q,\ldots ,p}\left(z^{\left(q\right)}\otimes y^{\left(q-1\right)}z^{\left(q-1\right)}\otimes\ldots \otimes y^{\left(p\right)}\right)z^{\left(p\right)}\otimes \ldots \otimes y^{\left(0\right)}z^{\left(0\right)}\right). \end{equation*} } \end{prop}

  {𝕃 L : Type*} [Fintype 𝕃] [DecidableEq 𝕃]
  (CF : 𝕃 → Type*) [∀ i, AddCommGroup (CF i)]
  (Q : Quiver 𝕃) (Λ : Type*) [Ring Λ]
  (ΛQ : Type*) [Ring ΛQ] [Algebra Λ ΛQ]
  (Λoplus : Submodule Λ ΛQ)
  (CF_L : CF L)
  (A : Type*) [NonUnitalNonAssocSemiring A]
  (A_inf : A →+* ΛQ)
  (A_unit : A)
  (m_k : ℕ → (Fin k → A) → A)
  (b : ΛQ)
  (W : ΛQ)
  (R : Ideal ΛQ)
  (Å : Type*) [Ring Å] [Algebra ΛQ Å] [Algebra Λ Å]
  (hΛoplus : Λoplus = ⊤)
  (hR : R = ⊤)
  (hW : W = 0)
  (hA : A_unit = 1)
  (hm_k : ∀ k, ∀ f : Fin k → ΛQ, ∀ X : Fin k → CF L,
    m_k k (fun i => A_inf (f i) * X i) = (∏ i : Fin k, f i) * m_k k X)
  (hb : b = ∑ l : CF L, b_l * l)
  (hCF_L : CF L = ⊤)

    (∀ (L1 L2 : 𝕃), ∃ (CF' : Type*) [AddCommGroup CF'],
      CF' ≃a[Λ] (Å ⊗[Λ] CF (L1 : 𝕃)))

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{construction} \label{constr:nc-single} \begin{enumerate} \item Associate a quiver $Q$ to $\mathrm{CF}^1(\mathbb{L})$. Namely, each component of (the domain of) $\mathbb{L}$ is associated with a vertex, and each generator in $\mathrm{CF}^1(\mathbb{L})$ is associated with an arrow. \item Extend the Fukaya algebra $A$ of $\mathbb{L}$ over the path algebra $\Lambda Q$ and obtain a non-commutative $A_\infty-$algebra $$\tilde{A}^\mathbb{L} = \Lambda Q\otimes_{\Lambda^\oplus} \mathrm{CF}(\mathbb{L}),$$ whose unit is $1_{\mathbb{L}} = \sum 1_{L_i}$. $\Lambda^\oplus \subset \Lambda Q$ denotes $\bigoplus_i \Lambda \cdot e_i$ where $e_i$ are the trivial paths at vertices of $Q$. The fibered tensor product means that an element $a \otimes X$ is non-zero only when tail of $a$ corresponds to the source of $X$. The $A_\infty$ operations are defined by \begin{equation} \label{eq:mk} m_k (f_1 X_1,\ldots,f_k X_k) := f_k \ldots f_1 \, m_k (X_1,\ldots,X_k) \end{equation} where $X_l \in \mathrm{CF}(\mathbb{L})$ and $f_l \in \Lambda Q$. \item Extend the formalism of bounding cochains of \cite{FOOO} over $\Lambda Q$. Namely, we take \begin{equation} \label{eq:b} b = \sum_l b_l B_l \end{equation} where $B_l$ are the generators of $\mathrm{CF}^1(\mathbb{L})$, and $b_l$ are the corresponding arrows in $Q$. Then define the deformed $A_\infty$ structure $m_k^{b}$ as in \cite{FOOO} and via Equation \eqref{eq:mk}. \item Quotient out the quiver algebra by the two-sided ideal $R$ generated by coefficients of the obstruction term $m_0^b$, so that $m_0^b = W\cdot 1_\mathbb{L}$ over $$\mathbb{A} := \Lambda Q/R.$$ $\mathbb{A}$ is called the noncommutative space of weakly unobstructed deformations of $\mathbb{L}$. We call $(\mathbb{A},W)$ to be a noncommutative localized mirror of $X$ probed by $\ma

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{example} When $X$ is a symplectic surface, any compact oriented immersed curve (together with a weak bounding cochain) is an object inside $\mathrm{Fuk}(X)$. The generators $(p_-,p_+)$ and $(p_+,p_-)$ can be visualized as angles at self-intersection points $p$, see Figure \ref{fig:angles}. The parity of degrees of generators are determined by orientation as shown in the figure. \begin{figure}[htb!] \centering \includegraphics[scale=0.6]{angles.pdf} \caption{Each transverse intersection point corresponds to two Floer generators.} \label{fig:angles} \end{figure} For surfaces, we will use the following sign rule for a holomorphic polygon bounded by $\mathbb{L}$ constructed by Seidel \cite{Seidel-book}. The spin structure is given by fixing spin points (marking where the non-triviality of the spin bundle occurs) in (the domain of) $\mathbb{L}$. Denote the input angles of the polygon $P$ by $X_1,\ldots,X_k$, and the output angle by $X_0$. If there is no spin point on the boundary of $P$ and the orientations of all edges of $P$ agree with that of $\mathbb{L}$, then the contribution of $P$ (via output evaluation) takes a positive sign. Otherwise, disagreement of the orientations on $\widearc{X_i X_{i+1}}$, for $i=2,\ldots,k-1$, affects the sign by $(-1)^{|X_i|}$. Whether the orientation on $\widearc{X_1 X_2}$ agrees with $\mathbb{L}$ or not is irrelevant. If the orientations are opposite on $\widearc{X_0 X_1}$, then we multiply by $(-1)^{|X_1| + |X_0|}$. Finally, we multiply by $(-1)^{l}$ where $l$ is the number of times $\partial P$ passes through the spin points. \end{example}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{thm:one-side inverse}
	Suppose $\alpha \in \mathbb{U}$ and $\beta \in \mathbb{U}^*$ are of degree 0 satisfying $\hat{m}_1^{b_1,b_2}(\alpha)=0$, $\hat{m}_1^{b_2,b_1}(\beta)=0$, and $\hat{m}_2^{b_2,b_1,b_2}(\beta,\alpha)=1_{\mathbb{L}_2}$.  Then the natural transformation $\mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_2,b_2)} \to \mathscr{F}^\mathbb{U} \circ \mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_1,b_1)}$ has a left inverse, i.e. $$\mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_2,b_2)} \to \mathscr{F}^\mathbb{U} \circ \mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_1,b_1)} \to \mathscr{F}^\mathbb{U} \circ \mathscr{F}^{\mathbb{U}^*}\circ \mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_2,b_2)} \to \mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_2,b_2)}$$ is homotopic to the identity natural transformation.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{thm: proj res}
	Assume that $\mathbb{U}$ has cohomology concentrated in the highest degree, that is, $\mathbb{U}$ is a projective resolution.  Then $\mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_2,b_2)}(L)$ is quasi-isomorphic to $\mathscr{F}^\mathbb{U} \circ \mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_1,b_1)}(L)$ for each object $L$, and $\mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_2,b_2)}(HF(L_0, L_1))$ is quasi-isomorphic to $\mathscr{F}^\mathbb{U} \circ \mathscr{F}^{(\mathbb{L}_1,b_1)}(HF(L_0, L_1))$ for all $L_0, L_1$.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lemma} \label{lem:loop} Suppose $\phi \in (\mathcal{A}_k \cdot e_{i_1}^{Q_k}\otimes e_{i_0}^{Q_j}\mathcal{A}_j) \otimes_{(\Lambda^\oplus)_k\otimes (\Lambda^\oplus)_j} \mathrm{CF}^\bullet(\mathcal{L}_j,\mathcal{L}_k)$, where $e_{i_1}^{Q_k}$ and $e_{i_0}^{Q_j}$ are the trivial paths at the $i_1$-vertex in $Q_k$ and $i_0$-vertex in $Q_j$ respectively. Then the coefficient of each output $P \in \mathrm{CF}^{\bullet}(\mathcal{L}_j,\mathcal{L}_k)$ in $m_1^{b_j,b_k}(\phi)$ belongs to $e_{h(P)} \cdot \mathcal{A}_k \cdot e_{i_1}^{Q_k} \otimes e_{i_0}^{Q_j} \cdot \mathcal{A}_j \cdot e_{t(P)}$. Similarly, let in addition that $\psi \in (\mathcal{A}_l \cdot e_{i_3}^{Q_l}\otimes e_{i_2}^{Q_k}\mathcal{A}_k) \otimes_{(\Lambda^\oplus)_l\otimes (\Lambda^\oplus)_k} \mathrm{CF}^\bullet(\mathcal{L}_k,\mathcal{L}_l)$. 		\item Fix a topological space $B$ and an open cover with $N$ open sets.  Moreover, fix a sheaf of algebras over each open set $U_j$ which is given by localizations of $\mathcal{A}_j$.
		For each $j=1,\ldots,N$, fix a vertex $v^{(j)} \in Q^{(j)}$.  Moreover, we fix $\alpha_{jk} \in \mathrm{CF}_{U_{jk}}^0((\mathcal{L}_j,b_j),(\mathcal{L}_k,b_k))$.
		\item Solve for gluing maps $G_{kj}:\mathcal{A}_{j}|_{U_{jk}} \to \mathcal{A}_{k}|_{U_{jk}}$ and gerbe terms $c_{jkl}(v)$ that define an algebroid stack $\mathcal{X}$ over $B$, such that the collection of $\alpha_{jk}$ satisfies \eqref{eq:closed} and \eqref{eq:cocycle-alpha} using $m_{1,\mathcal{X}}^{b_j,b_k}$ and $m_{2,\mathcal{X}}^{b_j,b_k,b_l}$.
	\end{enumerate}

entries.lean_statement: theorem example_94 {n : ℕ} {Λ : Type*} [Field Λ] (A B : Matrix (Fin n) (Fin n) Λ) (hA : A ≠ 0) (hB : B ≠ 0) : val A + val B ≤ val (A * B)

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Consider the algebra of $\Lambda$-valued $n$-by-$n$ matrices. Define
	$$ \mathrm{val} (A) := \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (\mathrm{tr} \, (AA^*)) $$
	where $A^*$ denotes the conjugate transpose of $A$.
	Explicitly, writing each non-zero matrix elements as $a_{ij} = T^{E_{ij}} c_{ij} (1 + o(T))$ where $E_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}$, $c_{ij} \in \mathbb{C}^\times$, and $o(T) \in \Lambda_+$, we have
	\begin{align*}
		\mathrm{val}_\Lambda (\mathrm{tr} \,(AA^*)) =& \mathrm{val}_\Lambda \sum_{i,j} a_{ij} \bar{a_{ij}} \\
		=& \mathrm{val}_\Lambda \sum_{i,j: a_{ij} \not=0} T^{2 E_{ij}} |c_{ij}|^2 (1 + o(T))(1 + \bar{o}(T)) \\
		=& 2 \min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (a_{ij})
	\end{align*}
	where the last equality holds because $|c_{ij}|^2 > 0$. 
	In particular, $\mathrm{val}(A)=+\infty$ if and only if $A=0$.
	Thus $\mathrm{val} A = \min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda a_{ij}$.
	In other words, $\mathrm{val} A$ is the maximal number such that $A / T^{\mathrm{val} A}$ has every entry in $\Lambda_{\geq 0}$.
	
	It is obvious that $\mathrm{val}(A)=\infty$ if and only if $A=0$.
	Now we check the conditions $\mathrm{val}(AB) \geq \mathrm{val}(A) + \mathrm{val}(B)$ and $\mathrm{val}(A+B) \geq \min(\mathrm{val}(A),\mathrm{val}(B))$. They are obvious if one of the matrices is zero, so let's assume $A\not=0$ and $B\not=0$. Let's write $A = T^{\mathrm{val} A} A_0$ and $B = T^{\mathrm{val} B} B_0$ where $A_0$ and $B_0$ have every entry in $\Lambda_{\geq 0}$ and at least one entry in each matrix has valuation zero. Then $AB = T^{\mathrm{val} A + \mathrm{val} B} (A_0 B_0)$ and $A_0 B_0$ has every entry in $\Lambda_{\geq 0}$. Thus $\mathrm{val} (AB) \geq \mathrm{val} A + \mathrm{val} B$.
	
	For the second condition,
	\begin{align*}
		\mathrm{val}(A+B) =& \min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda(a_{ij} + b_{ij}) \\
		\geq & \min_{i,j} \min(\mathrm{val}_\Lambda (a_{ij}), \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (b_{ij})) \\
		= & \min (\min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (a_{ij}), \min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (b_{ij})) \\
		= & \min (\mathrm{val}A, \mathrm{val}B).
	\end{align*}

assumptions: Define $$ \mathrm{val} (A) := \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (\mathrm{tr} \, (AA^*)) $$ where $A^*$ denotes the conjugate transpose of $A$.

Explicitly, writing each non-zero matrix elements as $a_{ij} = T^{E_{ij}} c_{ij} (1 + o(T))$ where $E_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}$, $c_{ij} \in \mathbb{C}^\times$, and $o(T) \in \Lambda_+$, we have \begin{align*} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (\mathrm{tr} \,(AA^*)) =& \mathrm{val}_\Lambda \sum_{i,j} a_{ij} \bar{a_{ij}} \\ =& \mathrm{val}_\Lambda \sum_{i,j: a_{ij} \not=0} T^{2 E_{ij}} |c_{ij}|^2 (1 + o(T))(1 + \bar{o}(T)) \\ =& 2 \min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (a_{ij}) \end{align*} where the last equality holds because $|c_{ij}|^2 > 0$.
They are obvious if one of the matrices is zero, so let's assume $A\not=0$ and $B\not=0$.
Let's write $A = T^{\mathrm{val} A} A_0$ and $B = T^{\mathrm{val} B} B_0$ where $A_0$ and $B_0$ have every entry in $\Lambda_{\geq 0}$ and at least one entry in each matrix has valuation zero.

For the second condition, \begin{align*} \mathrm{val}(A+B) =& \min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda(a_{ij} + b_{ij}) \\ \geq & \min_{i,j} \min(\mathrm{val}_\Lambda (a_{ij}), \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (b_{ij})) \\ = & \min (\min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (a_{ij}), \min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda (b_{ij})) \\ = & \min (\mathrm{val}A, \mathrm{val}B).
translation_statement_schema.claim: Consider the algebra of $\Lambda$-valued $n$-by-$n$ matrices. In particular, $\mathrm{val}(A)=+\infty$ if and only if $A=0$. Thus $\mathrm{val} A = \min_{i,j} \mathrm{val}_\Lambda a_{ij}$. In other words, $\mathrm{val} A$ is the maximal number such that $A / T^{\mathrm{val} A}$ has every entry in $\Lambda_{\geq 0}$. It is obvious that $\mathrm{val}(A)=\infty$ if and only if $A=0$. Now we check the conditions $\mathrm{val}(AB) \geq \mathrm{val}(A) + \mathrm{val}(B)$ and $\mathrm{val}(A+B) \geq \m

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{ex:val_ncC3}
	Let $\mathcal{A}^\hbar = \Lambda \langle w,y,x\rangle/\partial (yxw-T^{-3\hbar}xyw)$ be the noncommutative algebra given in Proposition \ref{prop:cA_1}. We have the relations 
	$$yx = T^{-3\hbar} xy, xw = T^{-3\hbar} wx, \textrm{ and } wy = T^{-3\hbar} yw.$$ 
	Given $v = (v_x,v_y,v_w) \in (\mathbb{R} \cup \{\infty\})^3$, we define a valuation $\mathrm{val}_v$ on $\mathcal{A}^\hbar$ as follows. For simplicity we write $\mathrm{val} = \mathrm{val}_v$ for a fixed $v$. First we set
	\begin{align*}
		\mathrm{val}(y) &= v_y, \\
		\mathrm{val}(x) &= v_x, \\
		\mathrm{val}(w) &= v_w.
	\end{align*}
	Moreover, we set $\mathrm{val}(y^kx^l) = k v_y + l v_x$, and similarly for $\mathrm{val}(x^kw^l)$ and $\mathrm{val}(w^ky^l)$.
	Then using the relations, we have $\mathrm{val}(x^{l_0}y^{k_1}x^{l_1}\ldots y^{k_m}x^{l_m}) \geq k v_y + l v_x$ where $\sum_{i=0}^m l_i = l$ and $\sum_{i=1}^m k_i = k$.
	For a general monomial with $k_y, k_x, k_w$ numbers of $y,x,w$ respectively, we consider
	$y^{k_y}x^{k_x}w^{k_w}$ if $k_x$ is maximal among $k_y, k_x, k_w$, $x^{k_x}w^{k_w}y^{k_y}$ if $k_w$ is maximal, and $w^{k_w}y^{k_y}x^{k_x}$ if $k_y$ is maximal. 
	We can check that such monomials have the minimal valuation among their permutations with the given relations. Then we define
	$$ \mathrm{val}(T^A y^{k_y}x^{k_x}w^{k_w}) = A + k_y v_y + k_x v_x + k_w v_w $$
	and similarly for $\mathrm{val}(T^A x^{k_x}w^{k_w}y^{k_y})$ and $\mathrm{val}(T^A w^{k_w}y^{k_y}x^{k_x})$.
	By this definition, the condition $\mathrm{val}(ab) \geq \mathrm{val}(a) + \mathrm{val}(b)$ holds for monomials $a,b$: let $a_0$ and $b_0$ be the monomials obtained from permutation of factors of $a$ and $b$ respectively such that $a_0$ and $b_0$ have the minimal valuation among all the permutations. 
	Then $\mathrm{val} (a) = \mathrm{val}(a_0) + 3k\hbar$ and $\mathrm{val} (b) = \mathrm{val}(b_0) + 3l\hbar$ for some non-negative integers $k,l$.
	Combining the two permutations, we have $\mathrm{val}(ab) = \mathrm{val}(a_0 b_0) + 3k\hbar + 3l\hbar$. 
	We can further permute $a_0b_0$ to achieve a monomial that has the minimal valuation which equals $\mathrm{val}(a_0) + \mathrm{val}(b_0)$.
	Thus $\mathrm{val}(a_0 b_0) \geq \mathrm{val}(a_0) + \mathrm{val}(b_0)$.
	Combining, we get
	$$ \mathrm{val}(ab) = \mathrm{val}(a_0 b_0) + 3k\hbar + 3l\hbar \geq \mathrm{val}(a_0) + \mathrm{val}(b_0) + 3k\hbar + 3l\hbar = \mathrm{val}(a) + \mathrm{val}(b). $$
	
	For a polynomial in $\mathcal{A}^\hbar$, we define its valuation being the minimum valuation among all of its monomial terms. Then a polynomial can be written as $P = P_0 + P_1$, where $P_0$ consists of all the terms with valuation being $\mathrm{val}(P)$ and $\mathrm{val} P_1 > \mathrm{val} P$. For two polynomials $P, Q$, we write
	$$ PQ = (P_0+P_1)(Q_0+Q_1) = P_0 Q_0 + P_0 Q_1 + P_1 Q_0 + P_1 Q_1 $$
	and so $\mathrm{val}(PQ) = \mathrm{val}(P_0Q_0)$. Every term in the polynomial expansion of $P_0 Q_0$ has valuation $\geq \mathrm{val}(P_0) + \mathrm{val}(Q_0) = \mathrm{val}(P) + \mathrm{val}(Q)$. Thus $\mathrm{val}(PQ) = \mathrm{val}(P_0Q_0) \geq \mathrm{val}(P) + \mathrm{val}(Q)$.
	
	The other two conditions, namely $\mathrm{val}(a+b) \geq \min(\mathrm{val}(a),\mathrm{val}(b))$ and $\mathrm{val}(a)=\infty$ if and only if $a=0$, are standard and easy to check.
	
	Given $v \in (\mathbb{R}\cup \{\infty\})^3$, we have the non-Archimedean norm $\norm{a}_v := e^{-\mathrm{val}_v(a)}$ on $\mathcal{A}^\hbar$. 
	Then we define $\overline{\mathcal{A}^\hbar}^v$ to be the subalgebra of formal power series in $\mathcal{A}^\hbar$ which are convergent with respect to this norm. The fact that this is a subalgebra easily follows from Properties (1) and (2) of the norm.
	For an open subset $U \subseteq (\mathbb{R}\cup \{\infty\})^3$, we define the completion
	\begin{equation} \label{eq:completion}
		\overline{\mathcal{A}^\hbar}(U) := \bigcap_{v \in U} \overline{\mathcal{A}^\hbar}^v.
	\end{equation} 
	By definition $\overline{\mathcal{A}^\hbar}(U) \subset \overline{\mathcal{A}^\hbar}(V)$ if $V \subset U$, and this gives a sheaf over $(\mathbb{R}\cup \{\infty\})^3$ which we denote by $\overline{\mathcal{A}^\hbar}$.

Then using the relations, we have $\mathrm{val}(x^{l_0}y^{k_1}x^{l_1}\ldots y^{k_m}x^{l_m}) \geq k v_y + l v_x$ where $\sum_{i=0}^m l_i = l$ and $\sum_{i=1}^m k_i = k$.

\label{ex:val_ncKP2}
	Consider the quiver algebra $\mathbb{A}^\hbar = \Lambda Q/\partial \Phi$, where $Q$ is the quiver in Figure \ref{fig:C3Q3} and $\Phi = -T^{\hbar}(b_1c_3a_2+a_1b_3c_2+c_1a_3b_2)+(c_1b_3a_2+b_1a_3c_2+a_1c_3b_2)$. For instance, one of the relations is $c_1b_3 = T^\hbar b_1c_3$.
	
	Given $v = (v_a,v_b,v_c) \in (\mathbb{R} \cup \{\infty\})^3$, we define a valuation $\mathrm{val} = \mathrm{val}_v$ on $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$ as follows. The valuation of idempotents $e_i$ at the three vertices $i=1,2,3$ are defined to be $0$. We set $\mathrm{val}(a_i) = v_a$, $\mathrm{val}(b_i) = v_b$, $\mathrm{val}(c_i) = v_c$ for all $i=1,2,3$.
	For a monomial starting with vertex $i$ with $k_a, k_b, k_c$ numbers of $a,b,c$ respectively (where $a_1,a_2,a_3$ are considered to be $a$, and similar for $b$ and $c$), we consider $a_{i+k_c+k_b+k_a-1} \ldots a_{i+k_c+k_b} b_{i+k_c+k_b-1}\ldots b_{i+k_c}c_{i+k_c-1}\ldots c_i$
	if $k_b$ is maximal among $k_a, k_b, k_c$, and similarly for the remaining two cases by cyclic permuting $a,b,c$.
	We can check that such monomials have the minimal valuation among their permutations with the given relations. Then we define
	$$ \mathrm{val}(T^A a_{i+k_c+k_b+k_a-1} \ldots a_{i+k_c+k_b} b_{i+k_c+k_b-1}\ldots b_{i+k_c}c_{i+k_c-1}\ldots c_i) = A + k_c v_c + k_b v_b + k_a v_a $$
	and similarly for the other two cases.
	As in Example \ref{ex:val_ncC3}, we can check that this defines a valuation on $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$.
	We have the sheaf of convergent series $\overline{\mathbb{A}^\hbar}$ over $(\mathbb{R}\cup \{\infty\})^3$.

The valuation of idempotents $e_i$ at the three vertices $i=1,2,3$ are defined to be $0$. We set $\mathrm{val}(a_i) = v_a$, $\mathrm{val}(b_i) = v_b$, $\mathrm{val}(c_i) = v_c$ for all $i=1,2,3$. We can check that such monomials have the minimal valuation among their permutations with the given relations. Then we define $$ \mathrm{val}(T^A a_{i+k_c+k_b+k_a-1} \ldots a_{i+k_c+k_b} b_{i+k_c+k_b-1}\ldots b_{i+k_c}c_{i+k_c-1}\ldots c_i) = A + k_c v_c + k_b v_b + k_a v_a $$ and similarly for the other two cases. As in Example \ref{ex:val_ncC3}, we can check that this defines a valuation on $\mathbb{A}^\hbar$. theorem example_101
    (Λ : Type*) [Field Λ]
    (B : Set ℝ) (hB : B = Set.Ico 0 1)
    (val : PolynomialAlgebra Λ → ℝ → ℝ)
    (h_val : ∀ (p : PolynomialAlgebra Λ) (b : ℝ), b ∈ B → val p b = -Real.log b)
    (T : ℝ) (hT : T > 0)
    (transition : PolynomialAlgebra Λ → PolynomialAlgebra Λ)
    (h_transition : ∀ (z : PolynomialAlgebra Λ), transition z = T^B * z⁻1)
    (completed_ring_x : CompletedLocalRing Λ)
    (completed_ring_z : CompletedLocalRing Λ)
    (h_completed : ∀ (f : PolynomialAlgebra Λ), ‖f‖_b = ‖transition f‖_(e^(-B) * b⁻1)) :
    ∃ (stack : Type*), Nonempty stack

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Consider the polynomial algebra $\Lambda[x]$ and $B = [0,1)$. For each $b \in B$, we assign $\mathrm{val}(x) := -\log b \in (0,+\infty]$ which gives a valuation $\mathrm{val}_b$ on $\Lambda[x]$. Then we take the completed local ring $\overline{\Lambda[x]}^B_{\geq 0}$ which consists of all series that are convergent and valued in $\Lambda_{\geq 0}$ with respect to $\mathrm{val}_b$ for all $b \in B$.
	
	Now consider two copies $\Lambda[x]$ and $\Lambda[z]$ with the transition map $x \mapsto T^B z^{-1}$ where $B>0$ is fixed. They are both over the interval $[0,1)$. The transition map gives $\mathrm{val} (x) = B - \mathrm{val} (z)$. Since $\mathrm{val} (x) > 0$, $\mathrm{val} (z) < B$. In other words, we glue the two intervals by the transition map $b_x = e^{-B} b_z^{-1}$ where the overlapping region is $(e^{-B},1)$ in each of the two intervals. They glue to a closed interval.
	
	By construction $\norm{f(x)}_{b_x} = \norm{f(T^B z^{-1})}_{e^{-B} b_z^{-1}}$ for any polynomial $f$. In the overlapping region, we take the completed local ring $\overline{\Lambda[x,x^{-1}]}^{(e^{-B},1)}_{\geq 0} \cong \overline{\Lambda[z,z^{-1}]}^{(e^{-B},1)}_{\geq 0}$. We get a non-Archimedean algebroid stack (namely a projective line) over the closed interval.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{example} Consider the noncommutative $K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$ glued from three affine charts as given by Equation \eqref{eq:relation_between_Seidel}. Here, the valuations for the variables (such as $v_{x_1}, v_{y_1}, v_{w_1}$) are taken in $\mathbb{R} \cup \{+\infty\}$. Taking $e^{-v}$, the corresponding base is glued by three copies of $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^3$ via the equations \begin{equation}\label{eq:gluing_ncKP2} \begin{cases} X_1 \mapsto e^{B+\hbar}Z_2^{-1}\\ Y_1 \mapsto e^{\frac{B}{2}+2 \hbar}Y_2 Z_2^{-1}\\ W_1 \mapsto e^{-\frac{3B}{2}-9\hbar}W_2 Z_2^{3} \end{cases} \begin{cases} Y_2 \mapsto e^{B+\hbar}X_3^{-1}\\ Z_2 \mapsto e^{\frac{B}{2}+2\hbar}Z_3 X_3^{-1}\\ W_2 \mapsto e^{-\frac{3B}{2}-9\hbar}W_3 X_3^{3} \end{cases} \begin{cases} Z_3 \mapsto e^{B+\hbar}Y_1^{-1}\\ X_3 \mapsto e^{\frac{B}{2}+2 \hbar}X_1 Y_1^{-1}\\ W_3 \mapsto e^{-\frac{3B}{2}-9\hbar}W_1 Y_1^{3}. \end{cases} \end{equation} where $X_1 = e^{-v_{x_1}}$ and so on. (Note that these are now commutative coordinates of $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^3$.) The base is homeomorphic to a toric polytope of $K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$. The geometric charts coming from Floer theory to be considered in the next subsection restrict $X_1 < 1$ (and similarly for other variables) which give three disjoint subsets $[0,1)^3$ in the base. We have another chart given by the quiver algebra (the nc resolution) in Example \ref{ex:val_ncKP2}, which is glued to the above three charts via Equation \eqref{eq:Seidel_Lag}. Let $\alpha = e^{-v_a}, \beta = e^{-v_b}, \gamma = e^{-v_c}$. Here we take $(\alpha,\beta,\gamma) \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^3 - \{(0,0,0)\}$. This is homeomorphic to the toric cone of $\mathbb{C}^3/\mathbb{Z}_3$ with the origin removed. Then the gluing for the base is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Seidel_Lag} \begin{cases} X

[Lemma 10.13 in \cite{CHL-nc}]
	Consider the formal nc deformation parameter $\boldsymbol{b} = \sum_{i=1}^3 a_i A_i+b_iB_i+c_iC_i$ of $\mathbb{L}$, where $A_i, B_i, C_i$ are generators of $\mathrm{CF}^1(\mathbb{L})$ and $a_i,b_i,c_i$ are the corresponding quiver arrows.  The nc unobstructed deformation space is $\mathbb{A}^\hbar = \Lambda Q/\langle\partial \Phi \rangle$, where $Q$ is the quiver in Figure \ref{fig:C3Q3}, $\Phi = -T^{\hbar}(b_1c_3a_2+a_1b_3c_2+c_1a_3b_2)+(c_1b_3a_2+b_1a_3c_2+a_1c_3b_2)$ and $\partial$ denotes the cyclic derivative.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{prop:isomorphism_KP2}
	There exist preisomorphism pairs between $(\mathbb{L}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_0)$ and $(S_i,\boldsymbol{b}_i),i=1,2,3$:
	$$\alpha_i \in CF_{\mathbb{A}_0^\hbar(U_{0i})\otimes \mathcal{A}_i^\hbar}((\mathbb{L}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_0),(S_i,\boldsymbol{b}_i)),\beta_i \in CF_{\mathcal{A}_i^\hbar \otimes \mathbb{A}_0^\hbar(U_{0i})}((S_i,\boldsymbol{b}_i),(\mathbb{L}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_0))$$
	and a quiver stack $\hat{\mathcal{Y}}$, whose charts are $\mathbb{A}_0^\hbar$ and $\mathcal{A}_i^\hbar,i=1,2,3$, that solves the isomorphism equations for $(\alpha_i,\beta_i)$ over the Novikov field $\Lambda$:
	\begin{align*}
		m_{1,\hat{\mathcal{Y}}}^{\boldsymbol{b}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_i}(\alpha_i) =& 0, m_{1,\hat{\mathcal{Y}}}^{\boldsymbol{b}_i,\boldsymbol{b}_0}(\beta_i) = 0;\\
		m_{2,\hat{\mathcal{Y}}}^{\boldsymbol{b}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_i,\boldsymbol{b}_0}(\alpha_i,\beta_i) =& 1_\mathbb{L}, m_{2,\hat{\mathcal{Y}}}^{\boldsymbol{b}_i,\boldsymbol{b}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_i}(\beta_i,\alpha_i) = 1_{S_i}.
	\end{align*}
	In above, $\mathbb{A}_0^\hbar(U_{0i})$ is the localization of $\mathbb{A}_0^\hbar$ at the set of arrows
	$\{a_1,a_3\},\{c_1,c_3\},\{b_1,b_3\}$ for $i=1,2,3$ respectively.  Moreover, $\boldsymbol{b}_i$ is restricted to the subset 
	$$\{\mathrm{val}(w_i)>B\} \subset \Lambda^3$$ 
	for $i=1,2,3$ and $\boldsymbol{b}_0$ is restricted to the subset 
	$$\left\{\mathrm{val} (b_1) > \mathrm{val} (a_1) + \frac{B}{2} + \hbar, \mathrm{val} (c_1) > \mathrm{val} (a_1) + \frac{B}{2}\right\}$$ 
	in order to define $G_{03}$ and $G_{30}$.  The cases for $G_{0i}$ and $G_{i0}$, $i=1,2$, are obtained by cyclic permutation.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{theorem}\label{prop:isomorphism_KP2} There exist preisomorphism pairs between $(\mathbb{L}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_0)$ and $(S_i,\boldsymbol{b}_i),i=1,2,3$: $$\alpha_i \in CF_{\mathbb{A}_0^\hbar(U_{0i})\otimes \mathcal{A}_i^\hbar}((\mathbb{L}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_0),(S_i,\boldsymbol{b}_i)),\beta_i \in CF_{\mathcal{A}_i^\hbar \otimes \mathbb{A}_0^\hbar(U_{0i})}((S_i,\boldsymbol{b}_i),(\mathbb{L}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_0))$$ and a quiver stack $\hat{\mathcal{Y}}$, whose charts are $\mathbb{A}_0^\hbar$ and $\mathcal{A}_i^\hbar,i=1,2,3$, that solves the isomorphism equations for $(\alpha_i,\beta_i)$ over the Novikov field $\Lambda$: \begin{align*} m_{1,\hat{\mathcal{Y}}}^{\boldsymbol{b}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_i}(\alpha_i) =& 0, m_{1,\hat{\mathcal{Y}}}^{\boldsymbol{b}_i,\boldsymbol{b}_0}(\beta_i) = 0;\\ m_{2,\hat{\mathcal{Y}}}^{\boldsymbol{b}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_i,\boldsymbol{b}_0}(\alpha_i,\beta_i) =& 1_\mathbb{L}, m_{2,\hat{\mathcal{Y}}}^{\boldsymbol{b}_i,\boldsymbol{b}_0,\boldsymbol{b}_i}(\beta_i,\alpha_i) = 1_{S_i}. \end{align*} In above, $\mathbb{A}_0^\hbar(U_{0i})$ is the localization of $\mathbb{A}_0^\hbar$ at the set of arrows $\{a_1,a_3\},\{c_1,c_3\},\{b_1,b_3\}$ for $i=1,2,3$ respectively. Moreover, $\boldsymbol{b}_i$ is restricted to the subset $$\{\mathrm{val}(w_i)>B\} \subset \Lambda^3$$ for $i=1,2,3$ and $\boldsymbol{b}_0$ is restricted to the subset $$\left\{\mathrm{val} (b_1) > \mathrm{val} (a_1) + \frac{B}{2} + \hbar, \mathrm{val} (c_1) > \mathrm{val} (a_1) + \frac{B}{2}\right\}$$ in order to define $G_{03}$ and $G_{30}$. semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Quantifiers: exists Assumptions: \label{thm: mirror KP2} There exists an algebroid stack $\mathcal{Y}$ over $\Lambda$ consisting of the following: \begin{enumerate} \item An open cover $\{U_i\}$ of polyhedral set $P$ of $K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$ for $i= 1, 2, 3$.; \item Sheaves of representations $G_{ij}:\mathcal{A}_{j}^\hbar|_{U_{ij}}\xrightarrow{} \mathcal{A}_{i}^\hbar|_{U_{ij}}$ satisfying the cocycle condition with trivial gerbe terms $c_{ijk}=1$ for $i,j,k \in \{1,2,3\}$. Conclusion: \item The collection of nc deformation spaces of Seidel Lagrangians $S_i$, $\mathcal{A}_i^\hbar$ over $U_i$ with coefficients $\Lambda$. % And NC mirror of the reference Lagrangian $\mathbb{L}$, $\mathcal{A}^\hbar_{0}:=\mathbb{A}_{0}^\hbar$ over $U_0$. \end{enumerate}

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{corollary} \label{thm: mirror KP2} There exists an algebroid stack $\mathcal{Y}$ over $\Lambda$ consisting of the following: \begin{enumerate} \item An open cover $\{U_i\}$ of polyhedral set $P$ of $K_{\mathbb{P}^2}$ for $i= 1, 2, 3$. \item The collection of nc deformation spaces of Seidel Lagrangians $S_i$, $\mathcal{A}_i^\hbar$ over $U_i$ with coefficients $\Lambda$. % And NC mirror of the reference Lagrangian $\mathbb{L}$, $\mathcal{A}^\hbar_{0}:=\mathbb{A}_{0}^\hbar$ over $U_0$. \item Sheaves of representations $G_{ij}:\mathcal{A}_{j}^\hbar|_{U_{ij}}\xrightarrow{} \mathcal{A}_{i}^\hbar|_{U_{ij}}$ satisfying the cocycle condition with trivial gerbe terms $c_{ijk}=1$ for $i,j,k \in \{1,2,3\}$. \end{enumerate} \end{corollary}

theorem theorem_110
    (Y : Type*) [Category Y] [HasTwistedComplexes Y]
    (A : Type*) [Category A] [DifferentialGraded A] [HasTwistedComplexes A]
    (U : TwistedComplex Y) (L1 L2 L3 : TwistedComplex A)
    (F : TwistedComplex A → TwistedComplex Y)
    (hF : ∀ L, F L ∈ TwistedComplex Y)
    (hF2 : F L2 ≅ sheafOfProjectiveSpace 2)
    (hF3 : F L3 ≅ sheafOfProjectiveSpace 2 ⊗ lineBundle (-1))
    (Ustar : TwistedComplex Y)
    (hFU : F L1 ∈ TwistedComplex Y)
    (hFUstar : F L1 ≅ Ustar)
    : ∃ (F' : DifferentialGraded A → TwistedComplex Y),
      F' L1 ∈ TwistedComplex Y ∧
      F' L2 ≅ sheafOfProjectiveSpace 2 ∧
      F' L3 ≅ sheafOfProjectiveSpace 2 ⊗ lineBundle (-1)

\label{proof: mirror_Seidel}

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{prop}[\cite{CHL}] \label{prop:cA_1} Consider the Seidel Lagrangian $S_1$ with the given orientation, fundamental class and spin structure in Figure \ref{fig:KP2_all}. Consider the formal nc deformations $\boldsymbol{b}_1 = w_1 W_1 + y_1 Y_1 + x_1 X_1$ of $S_1$. The noncommutative deformation space of $S_1$ is $\mathcal{A}_1^\hbar = \Lambda \langle w_1,y_1,x_1\rangle/\langle\partial \Phi_1 \rangle$, where $$\Phi_1 = y_1x_1w_1-T^{-3\hbar}x_1y_1w_1.$$ \end{prop}
